title: Apply: conservation of energy
description: Apply your knowledge of conservation of energy in this set of free practice questions.

assessment_items.question_content:
A child is at rest at the top of a waterslide. A graph modeling the child-Earth system’s starting kinetic energy $(\text{K})$ and gravitational potential energy $(\text{U}_\text{g})$ is shown below.

 [[☃ image 1]]

The child slides down the waterslide. The zero reference level for gravitational potential energy in the child-Earth system is the surface of the water, as shown below.

[[☃ image 3]]

**Which graph could represent the energy of the child-Earth system when the child is at the position shown in the image above?**  
*Assume frictional forces are small enough to ignore.*

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt:
A bar graph with categories labeled K and U g. The y-axis is labeled energy (J). 5 horizontal lines divide the y-axis into equal segments. The y-axis is labeled, from bottom to top, 0, 100, 200, 300, 400, 500.
The bar above the U g category goes from zero to 500. There is no bar above the K category.
A girl has just left the end of a waterslide and is in midair above the water in a pool.  The surface of the water below the girl is marked with a horizontal dotted line labeled U g equals zero.

choices.content: ![The same bar chart from above now shows  500 J of K and 200 J of U g.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/fe5c7c90abb94552c7037782c6fd087c.svg)
![The same bar chart from above now shows  300 J of K and 100 J of U g.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f3089efed42a389b9842b1b99752bbe4.svg)
![The same bar chart from above now shows  400 J of K and 100 J of U g.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/74d7b50570a6f17e308ff352dd38b0d6.svg)
![The same bar chart from above now shows  500 J of K and 0 J of U g.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/fc26f61767541c7fddc77a187bff174b.svg)

[[☃ image 4]]

**Which graph could represent the energy of the child-Earth system when the child reaches the surface of the water?**  
*Assume frictional forces are small enough to ignore.*

[[☃ radio 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
A roller coaster moves slowly over the top of a hill and coasts to the bottom, where it moves faster. As it coasts, a roaring sound can be heard.

[[☃ image 1]]

Two students, A and B, observe this phenomenon. They make the following claims about the coaster as it moves down:

* Student A claims energy is transferred to the coaster.  
* Student B claims energy is transferred from the coaster.

**For each observation below, select whether it supports student A’s claim or student B’s claim.**

Observation | Claim supported
-|-
the wheels and track get warmer | [[☃ dropdown 1]] 
a roaring sound is heard as the coaster moves | [[☃ dropdown 2]]
the coaster is moving faster at the bottom of the hill than at the top | [[☃ dropdown 3]]
hints: Energy is conserved. So, for any increase in energy there must be a decrease in some other energy, and vice versa.

The higher an object’s temperature, the greater the object’s thermal energy. So when the wheels and track get warmer, their thermal energy increases. That means some other energy decreases. That energy could come from the coaster. So, this observation supports student B’s claim.

Sound is a form of energy that travels in waves. So, a sound being heard is evidence that some other energy decreases. That energy could come from the coaster. So, this observation supports student B’s claim.

Kinetic energy depends on mass and speed. The greater an object’s speed, the greater its kinetic energy. So, the coaster moving faster at the bottom supports student A’s claim.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

The answers are:

Observation | Claim supported
-|-
the wheels and track get warmer | student B's
a roaring sound is heard as the coaster moves | student B's
the coaster is moving faster at the bottom of the hill than at the top | student A's

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.alt: A roller coaster train near the top of a hill moves down the hill.

assessment_items.question_content:
A snowboarder starts from rest at the top of a hill and glides to the bottom, where he's moving fast. As he glides a scraping sound can be heard.

[[☃ image 2]]

Two students, A and B, observe this phenomenon. They make the following claims about the snowboarder as he moves down:

* Student A claims energy is transferred to the snowboarder.  
* Student B claims energy is transferred from the snowboarder.

**For each observation below, select whether it supports student A’s claim or student B’s claim.**

Observation | Claim supported
-|-
the snowboarder is moving faster at the bottom of the hill than at the top | [[☃ dropdown 3]]
the snow beneath the snowboard melts a little | [[☃ dropdown 1]] 
a scraping sound is heard as the snowboard slides | [[☃ dropdown 2]]
hints: Energy is conserved. So, for any increase in energy there must be a decrease in some other energy, and vice versa.

Kinetic energy depends on mass and speed. The greater an object’s speed, the greater its kinetic energy. So, the snowboarder moving faster at the bottom supports student A’s claim.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

The higher an object’s temperature, the greater the object’s thermal energy. When the snow beneath the snowboard melts, its temperature, and therefore its thermal energy, increases. That means some other energy decreases. That energy could come from the snowboarder. So, this observation supports student B’s claim.

Sound is a form of energy that travels in waves. So, a sound being heard is evidence that some other energy decreases. That energy could come from the snowboarder. So, this observation supports student B’s claim.

The answers are:

Observation | Claim supported
-|-
the snowboarder is moving faster at the bottom of the hill than at the top | student A's
the snow beneath the snowboard melts a little | student B's
a scraping sound is heard as the snowboard slides | student B's


[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.alt: A snowboarder glides down a snowy slope.

assessment_items.question_content:
A zipliner starts from rest at the top of a mountain, speeding up as he coasts downward. As he coasts, a whirring sound can be heard.

[[☃ image 3]]

Two students, A and B, observe this phenomenon. They make the following claims about the zipliner as he moves down:

* Student A claims energy is transferred from the zipliner.  
* Student B claims energy is transferred to the zipliner.

**For each observation below, select whether it supports student A’s claim or student B’s claim.**

Observation | Claim supported
-|-
a whirring sound is heard as the zipliner moves | [[☃ dropdown 2]]
the zipliner speeds up as he moves along the cable | [[☃ dropdown 3]]  
the cable and pulleys get warmer | [[☃ dropdown 4]]
hints: Energy is conserved. So, for any increase in energy there must be a decrease in some other energy, and vice versa.

Sound is a form of energy that travels in waves. So, a sound being heard is evidence that some other energy decreases. That energy could come from the zipliner. So, this observation supports student A’s claim.

Kinetic energy depends on mass and speed. The greater an object’s speed, the greater its kinetic energy. So, the coaster moving faster at the bottom supports student B’s claim.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

The higher an object’s temperature, the greater the object’s thermal energy. So when the cable and pulleys get warmer, their thermal energy increases. That means some other energy decreases. That energy could come from the zipliner. So, this observation supports student A’s claim.

The answers are:

Observation | Claim supported
-|-
a whirring sound is heard as the zipliner moves | student A's
the zipliner speeds up as he moves along the cable | student B's
the cable and pulleys get warmer | student A's

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.alt: A person hangs in a harness from a cable as he slides down the cable. The cable begins at the top of a mountain and stretches downward into the distance.

assessment_items.question_content:
A person is riding a bicycle on flat ground. The person slams on the brakes. This causes the brake pads to squeeze the tires which stops the wheels from rolling, as shown below.

[[☃ image 4]]

As the bike [[☃ definition 1]] to a stop, its tires and the ground get warmer. 

**What happened to the kinetic energy of the bike-person system?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt:
A bike's handlebars have a person gripping both brake levers. The brake levers and hands are circled in red.

The back wheel of a bike has brake pads pressed against the sides of the tire. The brake pads are circled in red.

choices.content: It was transferred to the ground through the person’s pulling force on the brake levers.
It was transferred to the surroundings by the ground’s friction force on the tires.
It was transferred to Earth’s gravitational field by the gravitational force on the bike.
It was destroyed as the bike skidded to a stop.

assessment_items.question_content:
A person is driving a car on flat ground. The person slams on the brakes, which causes the wheels to stop rolling, as shown below.

[[☃ image 4]]

As the car [[☃ definition 1]] to a stop, its tires and the ground get warmer. 

**What happened to the kinetic energy of the car-person system?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A back view of the bottom of a car's tire rolling on the road. The tire stops rolling and slides against the ground, leaving a mark.

choices.content: It was transferred to the ground through the person’s pushing force on the brake pedal.
It was transferred to the surroundings by the ground’s friction force on the tires.
It was transferred to Earth’s gravitational field by the gravitational force on the car.
It was destroyed as the car skidded to a stop.

assessment_items.question_content:
A blue [[☃ definition 1]] slides toward a red curling stone that's at rest. The blue stone hits the red one. The blue stone stops and the red one starts sliding, as shown below.

[[☃ image 1]] 

**What happened to the majority of the blue stone's kinetic energy?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: A **curling stone** is a large disk-shaped granite stone that's used in the sport of curling.

options.alt: A blue curling stone slides across ice toward a red curling stone that's at rest. It collides with the red curling stone. After the collision, the blue stone is at rest. The red stone is moving in the same direction and at about the same speed as the blue one was before the collision.

choices.content: It was transferred to the red stone through the blue stone's interaction with the red stone.
It was transferred to the surroundings by the friction force from the ice on the stone.
It was transferred to Earth’s gravitational field by the gravitational force on the stone.
It was destroyed when it hit the red stone.

assessment_items.question_content:
A wrecking ball hanging from a crane is pulled up and away from a wall by a cable. The cable is released and the ball swings down. The ball smashes a hole in the wall.

[[☃ image 5]]

**Analyze the energy of the ball-Earth system throughout this process to complete the table.**  
*Assume air resistance is small enough to ignore.* 

Event | Total energy of ball-Earth system
-|-
the cable raises the ball | [[☃ dropdown 1]]
the ball swings down after the cable is released | [[☃ dropdown 2]]
the ball smashes into the wall | [[☃ dropdown 3]]
hints: Energy is conserved. So, the ball-Earth system’s total energy can only change if energy is transferred to or from it by something from outside the system.

As the cable raises the ball, it exerts a force on the ball, lifting it farther from Earth’s surface. So, the cable adds gravitational potential energy to the ball-Earth system. The system’s total energy increases.

As the ball swings down, the gravitational force from the Earth transfers energy from the gravitational field to the ball, increasing the ball’s kinetic energy. Both the Earth and the ball are inside the system. So, the ball-Earth system’s total energy doesn’t change.

When the ball smashes into the wall, the wall exerts a force on the ball, transferring energy from the ball. Some of this energy is turned into sound and thermal energy. And some energy is transferred to pieces of the wall, making them move. So, the ball-Earth system’s total energy decreases.

The answers are:

Event | Total energy of ball-Earth system
-|-
the cable raises the ball | increases
the ball swings down after the cable is released | stays the same
the ball smashes into the wall | decreases

options.alt: A wrecking ball hanging from a crane is pulled back by a cable. It then swings down and smashes into a wall.

assessment_items.question_content:
A wrecking ball hanging from a crane is pulled up and away from a wall by a cable. The cable is released and the ball swings down. The ball smashes a hole in the wall.

[[☃ image 5]]

**Analyze the energy of the ball-Earth system throughout this process. Then match each event below with its associated energy change.**  
*Assume air resistance is small enough to ignore.* 

[[☃ matcher 1]]
hints: Energy is conserved. So, the ball-Earth system’s total energy can only change if energy is transferred to or from it by something from outside the system.

As the cable raises the ball, it exerts a force on the ball, lifting it farther from Earth’s surface. So, the cable adds gravitational potential energy to the ball-Earth system. The system’s total energy increases.

As the ball swings down, the gravitational force from the Earth transfers energy from the gravitational field to the ball, increasing the ball’s kinetic energy. Both the Earth and the ball are inside the system. So, the ball-Earth system’s total energy doesn’t change.

When the ball smashes into the wall, the wall exerts a force on the ball, transferring energy from the ball. Some of this energy is turned into sound and thermal energy. And some energy is transferred to pieces of the wall, making them move. So, the ball-Earth system’s total energy decreases.

The answers are:

Event | Total energy of ball-Earth system
-|-
the cable raises the ball | increases
the ball swings down after the cable is released | stays the same
the ball smashes into the wall | decreases

options.alt: A wrecking ball hanging from a crane is pulled back by a cable. It then swings down and smashes into a wall.

left: the ball swings down after the cable is released
the ball smashes into the wall

assessment_items.question_content:
A wrecking ball hanging from a crane is pulled up and away from a wall by a cable. The cable is released and the ball swings down. The ball smashes a hole in the wall.

[[☃ image 5]]

**Analyze the energy of the ball alone and of the ball-Earth system throughout this process. Then complete the table.**  
*Assume air resistance is small enough to ignore.* 

Event | Kinetic energy of ball | Total energy of ball-Earth system
-|-
the ball swings down after the cable is released | [[☃ dropdown 2]] | [[☃ dropdown 5]]
the ball smashes into the wall | [[☃ dropdown 3]] | [[☃ dropdown 6]]
hints: The ball alone can only have kinetic energy. Energy is conserved. So, the ball's kinetic energy can only change if energy is transferred to or from it by an interaction from outside the system.  This is also true for the ball-Earth system.
As the ball swings down, the gravitational force from the Earth transfers energy from the gravitational field to the ball. So, the ball's kinetic energy increases.
But the gravitational interaction between the ball and Earth is *inside* the ball-Earth system. So, the ball-Earth system’s total energy doesn’t change.

When the ball smashes into the wall, the wall exerts a force on the ball, transferring energy from the ball. Some of this energy is converted into sound and thermal energy. And some energy is transferred to pieces of the wall, making them move. So, the ball's kinetic energy decreases.  

Because the ball-Earth system contains the ball, the ball-Earth system's total energy decreases.

The answers are:

Event | Kinetic energy of ball | Total energy of ball-Earth system
-|-|-
the ball swings down after the cable is released | increases | stays the same
the ball smashes into the wall | decreases | decreases

options.alt: A wrecking ball hanging from a crane is pulled back by a cable. It then swings down and smashes into a wall.
